summary:
The abolition of the samurai class as a hereditary military caste, formalized by the Haitorei Edict of 1876, was one of the most radical social transformations of the [[event:2544|Meiji Restoration]], eliminating an institution that had defined Japanese society for centuries and creating both the modern national army and the grievances that fueled the [[event:2591|Satsuma Rebellion]] the following year.

The Meiji government had been progressively dismantling the foundations of samurai privilege since 1868. The feudal domain system was abolished in 1871, eliminating the basis of samurai stipends. A universal military conscription system was introduced in 1873, creating a modern army of commoners that superseded the samurai's exclusive claim to military service. The Haitorei Edict of 1876 prohibited the wearing of swords in public — a deeply symbolic blow to samurai identity, since the right to carry two swords had been the most visible marker of samurai status.

Samurai stipends were converted to government bonds in 1876, then the bond system was modified in ways that dramatically reduced samurai income. Many former samurai struggled economically in the new order. Some successfully transitioned into the new professions — the military officer corps, the bureaucracy, business, and education — that the Meiji state was creating. Others, unable or unwilling to adapt, provided the manpower for the Satsuma Rebellion.

The abolition of the samurai class did not mean the erasure of samurai cultural values from Japanese society. The Meiji state deliberately cultivated Bushido — a codified version of the samurai ethical tradition emphasizing loyalty, honor, and self-sacrifice — as a component of modern Japanese national identity, channeling the samurai ethos toward the new imperial armed forces and instilling it through the school curriculum.
